attributes.stem:
A 67-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-month history of progressive difficulty swallowing and 10 kg weight loss. He can now only manage liquids with difficulty. He has a medical history of hypertension and takes amlodipine. He has a 40 pack-year smoking history and drinks three cans of beer daily.

On examination, he appears cachectic. His pulse rate is 82/min, blood pressure 135/85 mmHg, respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, and temperature 36.8°C. The remainder of the examination is being performed to determine the extent of disease.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following findings would most strongly suggest metastatic disease?
options: Persistent hoarseness with vocal cord paralysis
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic features of oesophageal cancer - progressive dysphagia (initially to solids, then liquids) and significant weight loss. The presence of a left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy (Virchow's node) is highly specific for metastatic disease from gastrointestinal malignancies, particularly oesophageal cancer. This finding would indicate Stage IV disease and generally precludes curative treatment.

Persistent hoarseness with vocal cord paralysis may indicate recurrent laryngeal nerve involvement by the tumour but could also be due to chronic laryngeal irritation from smoking. While concerning, it does not definitively indicate metastatic spread.

Unilateral pupillary constriction with ptosis suggests Horner's syndrome from sympathetic chain involvement. While this indicates local invasion, it does not necessarily mean distant metastatic disease.

Left-sided pleural effusion with dullness might indicate pleural involvement. However, in the context of oesophageal cancer, this could be due to various causes including infection or local spread rather than distant metastases.

Tracheo-oesophageal fistula with aspiration suggests local invasion into adjacent structures. This indicates local disease progression but not necessarily metastatic spread.

The presence of Virchow's node is particularly significant as it helps guide management decisions between curative and palliative approaches. Other examination findings may indicate local spread but do not as reliably indicate distant metastatic disease.
learningPoints: Virchow's node (left supraclavicular lymphadenopathy) is a specific sign of metastatic disease in gastrointestinal malignancies.
Dysphagia to liquids is a late feature of oesophageal cancer suggesting significant luminal narrowing.
Weight loss and progressive dysphagia are the cardinal presenting features of oesophageal cancer.
references: Short MW, Burgers KG, Fry VT. Esophageal Cancer. Am Fam Physician [Internet]. 2017; 95(1):22-28. Available from: https://www.aafp.org/pubs/afp/issues/2017/0101/p22.html
Aghedo BO, Kasi A. Virchow Node. [Updated 2022 Sep 26]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2022 Jan. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK556100/

Renshaw AA, Nappi D, Sugarbaker DJ, Swanson S. Effusion cytology of esophageal carcinoma. Cancer [Internet]. 1997 Dec 25; 81(6):365-372. Available from: 10.1002/(sici)1097-0142(19971225)81:6<365::aid-cncr11>3.0.co;2-x. PMID: 9438462.
